coredata.dc:description: Extensive thermal runaway characterization is essential in battery development, requiring an analysis of multiple parameters for safety assessment. This paper presents an approach for a comprehensive thermal runaway characterization based on experimental data from accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) and discretized autoclave thermal runaway calorimetry (DATRC). The method is applied to lithium-ion and sodium-ion batteries in the cell formats 18650, 21700, and 4680. The comparative analysis regarding thermal stability identified LFP as the cathode material with the highest stability. Furthermore, the heat release of NMC-811 and NFM show similar results regarding normalized heat release and energy fraction, while the LFP cells release less heat, and the fraction can vary greatly. A comparison between mass and energy fractions revealed that mass loss correlates with the energy released via gas and ejecta, but mass and energy flow do not necessarily have to be directly proportional, which can result in differing mass and energy fractions. A venting gas analysis, indicating an increased gas production for NMC-811 and NFM compared to LFP, finalizes the safety assessment. Overall, the presented approach and results provide a procedure and data basis for an extensive assessment of battery cells during cell selection, paving the way for accelerated safety-related battery design.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Extensive thermal runaway characterization is essential in battery development, requiring an analysis of multiple parameters for safety assessment. This paper presents an approach for a comprehensive thermal runaway characterization based on experimental data from accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) and discretized autoclave thermal runaway calorimetry (DATRC). The method is applied to lithium-ion and sodium-ion batteries in the cell formats 18650, 21700, and 4680. The comparative analysis regarding thermal stability identified LFP as the cathode material with the highest stability. Furthermore, the heat release of NMC-811 and NFM show similar results regarding normalized heat release and energy fraction, while the LFP cells release less heat, and the fraction can vary greatly. A comparison between mass and energy fractions revealed that mass loss correlates with the energy released via gas and ejecta, but mass and energy flow do not necessarily have to be directly proportional, which can result in differing mass and energy fractions. A venting gas analysis, indicating an increased gas production for NMC-811 and NFM compared to LFP, finalizes the safety assessment. Overall, the presented approach and results provide a procedure and data basis for an extensive assessment of battery cells during cell selection, paving the way for accelerated safety-related battery design.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: We want to give any researcher access to our results without any limits. The measurement data of all ARC experiments are available via the following digital object identifiers (DOI): https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.14956641 (18650 cells), https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.7707929 (21700 cells), and https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.14956635 (4680 cells). For the data on the DATRC experiments, the authors refer to the results in the paper, as it is not possible to use the raw data without confidential information.

ce:section.ce:para: Electric vehicles are becoming increasingly established on the market and record steadily rising registration numbers [1,2]. Neverless, to further increase the market share, it is necessary to continuously increase customer acceptance. Therefore, the performance and costs must be improved while providing a high level of safety for the battery system at the same time. However, optimizing the battery system concerning one of these target characteristics is usually only possible at the expense of another characteristic [3]. While the battery system can be optimized in terms of performance or costs, the requirements regarding the safety of a battery system are legally stipulated in a series of tests in various international standards and regulations and are essential for the homologation of the electric vehicle (e.g. UL 2580-2020, SAE J2464-2021, UN 3481, GTR 20, UN ECE R100 and GB 38031-2020) [4–7]. In particular, the requirements regarding the mitigation of thermal runaway propagation specified in GB 38031-2020 [8] and ECE R100 [9] are very challenging as the system behavior must be predicted, and possible faults must be addressed in the early phase of battery development. This requires system integration according to the safety characteristics of the battery cell [10] and thus immediately requires detailed awareness of the safety characteristics of the battery cell for cell selection, which mainly depends on the cell chemistry, cell format, and cell size [3].

Concerning cell chemistry, the safety-related differences between lithium-ion batteries have been extensively studied at component [11–14], cell [15–19], and system level [10,20–23]. In particular, the significant difference between olivin-type (e.g., LFP) and metal-oxide nickel-rich ternary cathodes (e.g., NMC-811 or NCA) used in the automotive sector offers leeway in the battery safety design. At the component level, Li et al. [24] used DSC and mass spectrometry to investigate the thermal runaway mechanism of NMC-811 cathodes and observed a release of oxygen species (O
2
, O
2
-, O-), which react with the anode and combustible components of the electrolyte, resulting in increased heat release. Further studies by Noh et al. [11], Bak et al. [12] and Liu et al. [13] revealed that a higher nickel content in the stoichiometric composition of the cathode leads to a higher oxygen release and a lower initial phase transition temperature and, thus to lower thermal stability and higher heat release. The reduced cobalt content was identified as the principal reason for reduced thermal stability, as Co dominates the chemical and structural stability of NMC cathodes by occupying the tetrahedral sites, delaying oxygen release and thermal failure [13]. In contrast, LFP cathodes offer good safety properties due to their strong covalent and thermally stable P–O bonds and metal ions of Fe2+ and Fe3+ [14,15,25–27]. Subsequent studies scaling up the safety research from the component level to the full cell level and from the cell level to the system level confirmed these results and showed improved thermal runaway behavior and slower thermal propagation in the battery module with LFP batteries [10,20]. For this reason, many manufacturers use battery cells with LFP cathodes in their electric vehicles, such as the BYD Han [28] or a model variant of the Tesla Model 3 [29], to optimize cost and facilitate the safety-related system integration of the battery cell at the same time.

Besides the cell chemistries already mentioned, post-lithium cell chemistries like sodium-ion batteries are also increasingly penetrating the market (e.g., JAC Sehol E10X [30]), promising even lower costs compared to established cell chemistries in electric vehicles. Regarding safety, Xia and Dahn [31] analyzed the thermal stability of NaCrO
2
as a cathode material for sodium-ion batteries and reported excellent thermal stability with minimal oxygen and heat release. Other electrode materials for sodium ion batteries were analyzed and compared with electrode materials of lithium-ion batteries by Samigullin et al. [32]. The investigations revealed that the thermal stability of the cathode materials increases in the following order: NFM < NMC < NVPF < LFP, NVP < NVPO [32,33]. Concerning the anode materials, hard carbon, as a commonly used anode material in sodium-ion batteries, showed a lower decomposition temperature and higher thermal effect compared to graphite, used in most lithium-ion batteries [32]. Furthermore, Moshin et al. investigated the safety properties of NVP [34] and HC electrodes [34,35] and showed improved safety properties of NVP, as it does not exhibit the O
2
evolution issue observed typically in layered oxide materials. At the full cell level, however, there are only a few studies on the thermal runaway of sodium-ion batteries. Bordes et al. [36] investigated the venting gas emissions of an NVPF | hard carbon sodium ion battery, which indicated similarities to LFP chemistries in the nature and quantity of the emitted gas. Further investigations on the venting gas temperature and mass loss of NFM sodium-ion batteries are presented by Fedoryshyna et al. [37], revealing lower mass losses and venting gas temperatures due to the absence of gas ignition compared to lithium-ion batteries. Robinson et al. [38] performed a full cell level analysis focusing on the thermal runaway behavior of sodium-ion batteries. In accelerating rate calorimetry experiments, a layered oxide | hard carbon sodium-ion pouch cell was compared with an LCO | graphite lithium-ion pouch cell. The investigations revealed that the thermal runaway in the sodium-ion cell occurs at higher temperatures [38]. However, the thermal runaway mechanism in sodium-ion batteries remains similar to that of lithium-ion batteries. Sodium-ion batteries with NTM cathodes were also compared with lithium-ion batteries using accelerating rate calorimetry by Yue et al. [39]. The results indicated maximum temperatures and heating rates intermediate between the LFP and NMC batteries, questioning the safety advantages of sodium-ion batteries with NTM cathodes compared to lithium-ion batteries with LFP cathodes.

The review of previous research shows that the thermal runaway mechanism of lithium-ion batteries has been extensively investigated. On the other hand, investigations of sodium-ion battery safety behavior are mainly limited to the performance of the individual material components. Only a few studies deal with investigations at full-cell level. However, investigations at the full-cell level are essential to properly map further mechanisms of thermal runaway, such as internal short circuits, chemical crosstalks (e.g., reactions between anode and cathode [40]), and, in particular, the correct mass balancing of the reaction products and reactants. Furthermore, the previous investigations only allow limited comparability, as different measurement methods were used, which usually only allow analysis concerning thermal stability or heat release during the thermal runaway. For this reason, this work aims to develop a uniform methodology for an extensive thermal runaway characterization to enable a safety assessment for cell selection in the early stage of the battery development process. In this work, the safety assessment focuses on a comparative analysis between a commercially available sodium-ion battery and two lithium-ion batteries with cell chemistries established in the automotive sector.

In addition, a trend towards large-format cells (e.g., Tesla 4680 cell [41] or BMW ‘New Class’ [42]) can be observed, whereby production costs and system complexity can be reduced at the same time [43,44]. Leading to a significant increase in cell chemistries, formats, and sizes available on the market [45,46]. The large number of possible system variants complicates the general system design in the early phase of the development process. Therefore, the developed methodology is also applied to cells with similar cell chemistry but different energy content to analyze whether and how the safety properties of one cell chemistry can be transferred or extrapolated to other cells. Based on this, the thermal runaway behavior of fictive cells can be estimated, and thermal runaway models [47–52] can be parameterized to facilitate safety assessment in the early stage of the safety-related development process.

This work provides a detailed characterization of the thermal runaway behavior of lithium-ion and sodium-ion batteries. The characterization methodology aims to identify differences in the safety properties and their development over the energy content to enable a safety assessment in the early phase of safety-related battery development. To achieve these research goals, the main contributions of this study can be summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Methodology for extensive thermal runaway characterization In the presented methodology, the characterization of thermal runaway is divided into the sub-areas of thermal stability and heat release to determine the safety properties as accurately as possible. For this purpose, the thermal stability is analyzed in experiments in the ARC separately from experiments on heat release in the DATRC.

Comparative analysis between the thermal runaway characteristics of a sodium-ion chemistry and two lithium-ion chemistries Elaboration of different thermal runaway characteristics between a commercially available sodium-ion battery and two lithium-ion cell chemistries established in the automotive industry.

Analysis for scaling the thermal runaway characteristics via the energy content In addition, further cells in the formats 18650, 21700, and 4680 with different energy content are compared to analyze the transferability of the results to other cells with similar cell chemistry but different energy contents and cell sizes. This may allow the results for cells with similar cell chemistry to be easily estimated.

ce:section.ce:para: The article starts with the description of the experimental design in Section 2 presenting the cell selection and the methods used to characterize the thermal stability and heat release during thermal runaway. Subsequently, the results of the characterization of the thermal runaway behavior are presented and discussed by providing a safety assessment between the investigated cells in Section 3. Finally, Section 4 summarizes the main findings of the work.

The following section presents the investigated battery cells and experimental methods in more detail. Section 2.1 summarizes the cell selection and cell specifications. Thereafter, the experimental techniques to characterize the thermal runaway behavior are described. Starting with the characterization of the thermal stability in Section 2.2 concerning the accelerating rate calorimeter (ARC) experiments and followed by Section 2.3, focusing on the investigations in the discretized autoclave thermal runaway calorimeter (DATRC) to characterize the heat release during thermal runaway.

All relevant parameters of the battery cells used for this study are shown in Table 1 and differ mainly in terms of cell chemistry and cell size. The cell chemistries NMC-811 | graphite, LFP | graphite, and NFM | hard carbon were selected for the following investigations, as these cell chemistries are currently or may be frequently used in automotive applications in the future [45,46]. Please note that all cells are commercially available, and therefore, the cell chemistries are very similar but not identical. For example, there are minor differences in the silicon content in the anode within the NMC-811 | graphite cell chemistries, and differences in the composition of the electrolyte solvent are also to be expected. The energy content is scaled by the cell formats 18650, 21700, and 4680, whereby only the 18650 cell format is examined for the NFM | hard carbon, as no cells in the 21700 and 4680 formats were commercially available during the time of this study. Even if the external dimensions are identical, minor geometric differences between the cells cannot be avoided. While the structure of the 18650 and 21700 cell formats is very similar, the 4680 cell formats differ, for example, in the size and position of the safety vents. In addition, the NMC-811 | graphite cells in 4680 format were dismantled from a Tesla Model Y and have a tabless design, examined in more detail in an earlier study [41]. A detailed characterization of the investigated sodium-ion cell can be found in [54]. In addition to the data shown in Table 1, further information on the 21700 cells is also available in [19]. Please note that the minor differences in cell chemistry and size are unavoidable due to the commercial availability of the cells but contribute to the final assessment of the validity of the transferability of safety characteristics to battery cells with similar cell chemistries. Each cell is in a completely new state but is cycled ten times at a charge/discharge rate of C/3 between the respective voltage limits with a XCTS battery cycler (BaSyTec GmbH, Germany) before the experiment to verify the cell capacities and ensure a fully formed state with a well-defined SEI layer. Before each experiment, the cell was charged to a state of charge of 100%. All tests were carried out at this SOC in an unaged state, offering the greatest hazard potential and representing a worst-case scenario [55–58]. Hence, the quantitative results of the investigated cells in an aged state or other SOCs may deviate. According to the current literature, the deviations in aged battery cells are expected to result in less violent failing reactions, reduced maximal temperatures, lower vent gas volume, and lower mass losses with decreasing SOC and SOH [55–58].

Thermal abuse tests were carried out in the accelerating rate calorimeter to investigate the thermal stability of the cells. For the experiments in this study, a calorimeter of the type ES ARC (Thermal Hazard Technology, United Kingdom) was used for the 18650 and 21700 cells. Due to the capacity limits of the device under tests recommended by the manufacturer, the same calorimeter could not be used for the large-format 4680 cells. For this reason, a calorimeter of the type EVx ARC (Thermal Hazard Technology, United Kingdom) was selected for the cells in the format 4680. Both calorimeters were calibrated before the tests according to the manufacturer’s recommendations using aluminum dummies in the respective cell size and with the thermocouple setup used in the test. The calibration ensures an optimized heating power setting for uniform heating, which minimizes temperature gradients in the test specimen and, therefore, provides an accurate recording of the self-heating rate with all characteristic temperatures.

The schematic setup of an accelerating rate calorimeter is shown in Fig. 1(a). The battery cell is placed in the center of the calorimeter chamber and is surrounded by three heaters, which are integrated into the lid, bottom, and side wall of the calorimeter. A type N thermocouple with a accuracy of ± 1.5°C is attached to the battery cell. The sensitivity of both calorimeter types is 0.02°C min−1. The battery cells were measured in the calorimeter using the Heat-Wait-Seek (HWS) method to accurately characterize the characteristic temperatures and the reaction kinetics of exothermic reactions. The test sequence is shown in a flow chart in Fig. A.1. The HWS cycle starts with a heating step, which increases the test specimen temperature by 5°C. In the following waiting phase, a thermal equilibrium is created. At the end of this phase, exothermic self-heating is sought. If there is no significant self-heating, a new HWS cycle begins, and the sample continues to be heated successively in 5°C steps. In the case of exothermic self-heating above the sensitivity threshold value of 0.02°C/min, the calorimeter switches to exothermic control mode. In this phase, quasi-adiabatic conditions are established, resulting in no heat transfer between the test specimen and the calorimeter. The subsequent self-heating can be primarily attributed to exothermic reactions during the thermal runaway.

Fig. 2 provides sample data for an ARC experiment for better demonstration of the analysis. The analysis is partly based on the three characteristic temperatures
T
1
,
T
2
, and
T
3
according to Feng et al. [59].
T
1
signals the onset temperature of significant self-heating, defined in this work as a heating rate above 0.001°C s−1. This point is located at the end of the HWS phase in Fig. 2(a) and simultaneously marks the beginning of the quasi-adiabatic state in the calorimeter with accelerated self-heating.
T
2
is often simplified as TR trigger temperature, but since the entire heating process is referred to as TR, thus technically, there is no TR temperature. For this reason, the term temperature of critical self-heating and rapidly increasing temperature rate, as shown in Fig. 2(b), is preferred in this work. A general definition of
T
2
via a temperature rate does not exist in the literature, as the temperature rates during thermal runaway can differ immensely depending on cell chemistry and state of charge [19]. Moreover, the results can fluctuate significantly if the temperature rate changes abruptly or the reactions take place faster than the calorimeter can track. For these reasons,
T
2
is defined according to Ohneseit et al. [19] at a temperature rate of approximately 0.33°C/s (20°C/min), which corresponds to the maximum tracking rate of the calorimeter and thus ensures sufficient monitoring with reliable results. The third characteristic temperature
T
3
is the maximum temperature the battery cell reaches during thermal runaway. In addition to the characteristic temperatures, the temperature at which the battery cell bursts and outgasses under adiabatic conditions is also evaluated. The outgassing is noticeable in the temperature plot in Fig. 2(a) by a short drop in temperature due to the Joule-Thomson effect and is referred to as venting temperature
T
v e n t
. The final evaluation criterion for the tests in the ARC is the maximum heating rate
H
R
m a x
that the battery cell reaches during the thermal runaway in Fig. 2(b).

The test was performed twice for each cell in order to assess the reproducibility of the results. Please note, that the data for the cells in the 21700 format were previously published in a study by one author [19] and are reused for these investigations. However, the test procedure and the calorimeter used are identical to those used in this work.

Discretized autoclave thermal runaway calorimetry (DATRC) represents a fusion of fractional thermal runaway calorimetry (FTRC) [60] and autoclave calorimetry [61]. On the one hand, fractional thermal runaway calorimetry, introduced by NASA [62] and published in a scientific article by Walker et al. [60] in 2019, allows measuring the total heat output and its fraction. On the other hand, autoclave calorimetry reveals further insights into the gas release and improves energy conservation in an enclosed setup. As a synergy of both methods, DATRC enables simultaneous measurement of the heat and gas release of battery cells in thermal runaway.

Alongside determining the total heat output, DATRC and FTRC can also decouple heat remaining in the cell body (solid fraction) from heat associated with the ejected gas and particles (gas fraction). Therefore, these methods perfectly enhances the ARC experiments, as it is difficult to estimate the total heat release
Q
t o t , T R
in the ARC, as the heat release is often determined in a simplified manner via the mass of the cell
m
c e l l
, the heat capacity of the cell
c
p , c e l l
, and the temperature difference
Δ T
between the two characteristic temperatures
T
2
and
T
3
according to Eq. (1) [59,63].
(1)
Q
t o t , T R
=
m
c e l l
⋅
c
p , c e l l
⋅ Δ T

Since no consistent definition for
T
2
exists and
T
3
can only be determined inaccurately in most cases, as the thermocouple often loses contact with the cell during TR and the cell is cooled from a temperature above 350°C, the temperature difference can only be determined very poorly [64]. Furthermore, precise knowledge of the specific heat capacity poses a major challenge, as it is heavily dependent on the mass fraction of the respective components, such as the amount of electrolyte, and there is only a very limited number of research for the validity at high temperatures [65]. In addition, it is impossible to distinguish between ejected and non-ejected heat in the ARC experiments. By contrast, the reaction kinetics can be determined very well in the ARC experiments due to its high sensitivity and quasi-adiabatic conditions.

The DATRC experiments were executed at Akkuracy Battery Testing GmbH using a calorimeter setup consisting of two major assemblies, which separately determine the gas and solid fractions of the heat release.

The assembly for determining the solid fraction of the heat release is called Stage 0 and is illustrated in Fig. 1(b) for the experiments with the 4680 cells. The battery cell is braced between two copper half-shells with the use of six screws. The half-shells have an open and a closed side. The battery cell is inserted within the half-shells, with the vent of the cell facing the open side, to enable venting in the assembly for determining the gas fraction. All battery cells are thermally triggered, as thermal runaway may not be ensured in the case of nail penetration for LFP cell chemistries [19]. Therefore, two heating foils with a heating power of 150W each are attached to the surface of the 4680 battery cells, which results in a maximum heating power of 300W in total. Due to space constraints, only one heating foil with a maximum heating power of 150W was used between the battery cell and the half-shells for the 18650 and 21700 cells. The general setup is identical for all cells, except that the mass of the copper half-shells was adjusted according to the cell format’s expected heat release. Depending on the cell format, an individual number of temperature sensors were homogeneously distributed on the half-shells to ensure sufficient temperature monitoring.

The assembly for determining the gas fraction can be further subdivided into three stages and are referred to as Stage 1–3. Each stage consists of a copper pipe with a special heat exchanger design to absorb the heat from the ejected particles and emitted venting gas. The design has been slightly adapted in the stages to each cell’s expected amount of particles and vent gas to ensure adequate heat exchange. The temperature rise is monitored with a multitude of temperature sensors for each stage, which are evenly distributed in the stages. All assemblies are embedded in a thermal insulation with a low thermal conductivity to reduce heat exchange between the assemblies and the calorimeter housing.

Please note that the presented test setup is only suitable and designed for the investigated cell chemistries and cell formats. DATRC experiments are also possible with further battery cells in other cell formats or energy contents. However, this requires prior adjustments to the experimental setup depending mainly on cell geometry and expected energy release. For instance, Walker et al. [66] demonstrate an adaption of a FTRC setup to large-format prismatic battery cells.

Sample data for the temperature signals are shown in Fig. 3(a–k) for all stages and the thermal insulation. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the battery cells were triggered using a heating ramp with a constant temperature rate of 5°C/min according to the test sequence shown in Fig. B.1. The heating elements were switched off manually after detecting a significant temperature increase.

In analogy to the work of Walker et al. [60], the released heat is calculated via the temperature increase of the respective components. Therefore, the heat release is first determined separately for each component i of the calorimeter

However, the local gradients should be low as all components that contribute to calculating the heat quantity consist of copper to achieve the most accurate results. Copper has a very high thermal conductivity of 380–400

As the measurement setup does not represent a perfectly adiabatic environment, temperature-dependent conductive and convective heat losses via the insulation must also be considered. For this reason, the losses were determined via the cooling curve of all calorimeter components and compensated with a constant heat loss

When the total heat release reaches its maximum, the heat quantities of the individual components and insulations of Stage 0 can be summed up to determine the heat remaining in the cell body (solid component). Similarly, summing up the heat quantities of the components and insulations of Stages 1 to 3 leads to the heat quantity associated with the particles and the gas (gas fraction). The heat losses
Q

For determination of the gas release, the calorimeter housing is an autoclave sealed gas-tight, allowing the amount of vent gas

In the following, the findings of the thermal runaway characterization are presented. Section 3.1 starts with the thermal stability determined using the accelerating rate calorimetry. Subsequently, the results of heat release from the DATRC tests are considered in Section 3.2. Finally, the differences in mass loss and venting gas are discussed in Sections 3.3 and 3.4 to finalize the safety assessment.

Fig. 4 provides the results from the ARC tests used to analyze thermal stability. For the comparison between the different cell chemistries, the temperature rate in the exothermic phase is plotted over the temperature in Fig. 4(a). Analogously, the results of the transferability to other cells and scaling via the energy content are shown in Fig. 4(b).

The measurement results show good reproducibility. Although each test was carried out twice, the characteristic curves show good agreement and are difficult to distinguish from each other. In contrast, apparent differences can be seen depending on the cell chemistry in Fig. 4(a), as the curves show very different characteristics of the heating rate over the temperature. Within the same or similar cell chemistry, the characteristic curves also show mostly similar characteristic behavior, as illustrated in Fig. 4(b). It was expected that the differences in terms of cell size and, therefore, energy content would be small, as temperature gradients in the cell are minimal due to the uniform and slow self-heating under quasi-adiabatic conditions in the ARC. Nevertheless, it was found that slight manufacturing differences and minor differences in cell chemistry have no significant influence on thermal stability, so the results can be transferred to other cells with similar cell chemistry up to a certain degree of accuracy, thereby saving test capacities.

Considering the characteristic temperatures according to Feng et al. [59] in Table 2, significant self-heating of the 18650 cells begins at 96.2 and 96.3°C for the NMC-811 cells. For the other cell chemistries, significant self-heating starts significantly later at temperatures between 145.7–150.1°C for the NFM cells followed by the LFP cells between 151.9–167.7°C. This order can be explained by the SEI decomposition reactions, which are characteristic for this temperature range. Since the theoretically possible amount of reactants for the SEI decomposition reactions is directly proportional to the cell capacity, it is clear that the onset of significant self-heating in the LFP and sodium-ion cell chemistry occurs significantly later compared to the NMC-811 cell, but does not explain why the sodium-ion cell chemistry has slightly lower values for
T
1
compared to the LFP cell. Nevertheless, according to several studies [33,68], this can be explained by the nature of the SEI, which has a lower stability at higher temperatures in Na-based systems in contrast to its lithium counterpart, leading to an earlier heat release.

If the observations are extended to the other cell formats, it can be seen that the results within the LFP cells are confirmed, as all other temperatures for
T
1
are within the range already determined. In contrast, a closer look at all NMC-811 cells reveals a small shift to higher temperatures and, thus, a later onset of significant exothermic self-heating with larger cell formats. However, the root cause is not assumed to be the cell size or energy content, as this is not a conclusive explanation due to the very low temperature gradients and heating rates. Instead, the more likely reason is assumed to be the composition of the anode, as
T
1
is often associated with the decomposition and regeneration of the SEI at the anode [16,59]. The cause is, therefore, suspected to be the reduced thermal stability of blended silicon-graphite anodes compared to graphite anodes [69], whereby the balanced reaction of SEI decomposition and regeneration is accelerated and results in a higher heat generation [70]. This hypothesis is supported by the fact that the decreasing silicon content in the anode in Table 1 correlates with an increase of
T
1
. Smaller differences due to minor differences in the cell chemistry are therefore recognizable. Still, these are too small to affect the final safety behavior or the dynamics of the thermal runaway since the heating rate is too low and the characteristic curves already converge at higher temperatures and show a uniform behavior again.

The gaseous decomposition products of the decomposition of the SEI and the vaporization of the electrolyte above the boiling point of many solvents lead to an increase in pressure in the battery cell [71,72], causing it to outgas and open when the trigger point of the safety vent is exceeded. Venting occurs for the NMC-811 18650 and 21700 cells under adiabatic conditions in a temperature range between 115.8 and 129.6°C. The similar results for
T
v e n t
are not very surprising for the 18650 and 21700 cells due to their similar cell and vent design. However, the NMC-811 4680 cell vents significantly later at a temperature of 177.5°C or even higher. In test number 5, it was no longer possible to distinguish between the temperature drop and the strong exothermic phase near
T
2
. The late venting leads to higher internal pressures, which increase the potential risk for accelerated propagation due to side wall ruptures. To counteract this, Tesla uses a significantly thicker cell can (0.5 mm [41]) than conventional 18650 and 21700 cell cans (0.2 mm [73]). However, the benefit of delayed venting is still questionable, as many redundant TR detection methods are based on the detection of gas as a consequence of early venting and a pressure increase in the housing of the battery system [74,75], both will occur with a delay with this safety-related design and thus result in slower TR detection. The LFP cells show a very broad range for the venting temperature between 151.9 and 167.7°C. It is on the same level with the NFM cells with 163.9–165.7°C and, therefore, significantly higher despite a similar cell and safety vent design to the 18650 and 21700 NMC-811 cells. However, this is evident if one considers the reduced gas development due to the previously described varying amounts of reactants and boiling points of the electrolyte solvents.

The temperature rate increases exponentially for all cells until there is a rapid increase in the temperature rate in the 0.33°C/s region. The critical rapidly increasing self-heating occurs at different temperatures
T
2
depending on the cell chemistry. Within the NMC-811 cells,
T
2
shows a very reproducible behavior, whereby
T
2
lies between 181.2–186.1°C. A reproducible behavior between 232.9 and 240.8°C was also observed in the NFM sodium-ion cell chemistry.

Internal short circuits due to separator collapse are one of the reasons associated with the strong increase in the temperature rate [16]. Since similar polymer separators with minor differences are used in all the cells investigated, this does not explain the widely varying temperatures for
T
2
. In addition, the possible heating power due to an internal short circuit is estimated to be low, as the cell resistance is relatively high due to the separator shutdown and the decomposed and potentially dried-out electrolyte components [16,70]. Therefore, the internal short circuit is a further upstream process of thermal runaway, which favors other strongly exothermic decomposition reactions. However, one of the primary heat sources during thermal runaway is the chemical crosstalk between the cathode and anode [40,70]. This decomposition reaction is mainly driven by the release of oxygen at the cathode during the phase transition from the layered to the spinel structure at high temperatures, which fit well with the results for
T
2
[11,12,14]. The sodium-ion cell exhibits a very similar behavior, showing a reproducible rapid increase in the temperature rate. The similar behavior can probably be attributed to the cathode type since NFM is also a layered metal oxide cathode such as NMC-811. Thus, a similar crosstalk between anode and cathode could exist. The existence of a phase transition with oxygen release in NFM has already been reported by Xie et al. [76], observing the onset of the phase transition around 243°C, which fits well with the results obtained for
T
2
. In addition, Teng et al. [77] reported significant heat generation in the material combinations cathode and anode as well as cathode and electrolyte, which suggests a subsequent reaction of the oxygen with the electrolyte and the sodiated hard carbon anode, comparable to NMC cells.

The similar behavior leads to the assumption that many behaviors of the already well-studied NMC cathodes are transferable to NFM. For example, the thermal stability of the cathode material is strongly influenced by the stoichiometric composition of the cathode [11,12], which means that a higher nickel content in the NFM cathode to increase energy density could presumably also lead to a reduction in thermal stability with lower
T
2
.

Compared to the other cell chemistries, the LFP cells show less reproducible behavior in the
T
2
range. This applies to comparing different as well as identical LFP cells, leading to a wide range for
T
2
between 236.2 and 257.1°C. The origin of the different dynamics can be attributed to the better thermal stability of LFP due to their strong covalent and thermally stable P-O bonds, which inhibit rapid and high oxygen release [14,15,25–27]. The thermal runaway is, therefore, less dynamic and peaks at maximum heating rates between 6.2 and 30.4°C s−1. The maximum heating rates for the other cell chemistries are of different magnitudes, with 94.9–435.6°C s−1 for NMC-811 and 97.3–100.6°C s−1 for NFM. The fluctuations in the temperature rate are due to the dynamics of the thermal runaway. Please note that at this point, there are no longer quasi-adiabatic conditions in the calorimeter.

Finally, the results for
T
3
show a high variation for all cells, which highlights the problem already described in Section 2.3 and does not allow a valid interpretation of the maximum temperature during thermal runaway. As a result, the total heat release according to Eq. (1) can only be estimated with a very large error based on the data. For a complete safety assessment, DATRC tests in the following section supplement the characterization of the heat release, allowing a more precise determination and subdivision into a solid and gas fraction.

Fig. 5(a) presents each investigated battery cell’s heat release and fraction. No data is available for the LFP 18650 cell as no significant temperature increase in Stage 0 was observed, and therefore, no calculation of the heat release was possible. An additional LFP 4680 cell was measured as a substitute, and the results are shown in Fig. 5(c) compared to the other 4680 LFP cell in Fig. 5(b).

Depending on the cell chemistry, the investigated cells differ primarily in fraction and heat release. While all NMC-811 cells release over 87% of the energy via ejecta and gas, the fraction for the LFP cells varies greatly, with 26 to 39% at low and 88% at high mass losses, similar to the NMC-811 cells. Therefore, part of the cause can be seen in the mass ejection and is discussed in more detail in Section 3.3. Besides the quantitative differences in the heat release, the NFM cell exhibits an ejecta and gas fraction with 93% comparable to the NMC-811 cells. This reinforces the previous hypothesis that qualitative behavior during thermal runaway can be transferred to similar cathode structures, such as layered oxide cathodes. The total energy release of the NMC-811 cells is significantly greater than that of the LFP or NFM counterparts for each cell format. This is not surprising as the energy content of the NMC-811 cells is considerably higher. For this reason, the heat release is set in relation to the cell capacity and energy in Fig. 6.

Figs. 6(a and e) show that the total heat release for all cell chemistries can be estimated and scaled relatively well via the cell capacity and energy content. However, the scaling differs depending on the cell chemistry, with NMC-811 and NFM showing similar behavior and releasing between 17.6 and 19.5kJAh−1, corresponding to about 1.33 to 1.77 times the electrically stored energy. In contrast, the LFP cells release 10.0 to 11.9kJAh−1, leading to a heat output similar to the electrically stored energy. An attempt to scale the heat release independently of knowledge of the cell chemistry or cathode structure by the energy density is not possible, as shown in Figs. 6(c and g). Although the LFP cells confirm the trend that materials with a lower energy density also release less energy per capacity and energy, the NFM cell proves the opposite. In contrast, a method by Masalkovaitė et al. [80] scaled by the total ejected mass provides a better estimation independent of cell chemistry. However, this has only been shown for various cylindrical lithium-ion cells with layered metal oxide cathodes. Figs. 6(d and h) show that this relationship applies to other cylindrical batteries with other lithium-ion and sodium-ion chemistries. Nevertheless, it must be considered that this is only valid for cells with similar formats and vent designs, as these factors can strongly influence the total ejected mass. For example, prismatic cells, as studied by Hoelle et al. [61], have a mass loss between 22 and 67% with a total energy release of 15.6–22.8kJAh−1, which means that they do not fit into the previous results and a new characteristic curve would have to be formed for prismatic cells with similar formats and vent designs.

The results from the calorimeter setup are verified by comparing them with data from the literature. With an average heat release of 18.4kJAh−1, the results are in the middle of the previously measured ranges for the heat release of lithium-ion batteries with layered oxide cathodes according to Hoelle et al. [61] (15.6–22.8kJAh−1) and Walker et al. [60] (15.2–21.5kJAh−1). In addition, the results in Fig. 6 are compared with the data from the publicly available battery failure databank [78]. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, all lithium-ion cells in this database have exclusively layered metal oxide cathodes at the time of this work. A comparison of the cell chemistries LFP and NFM with literature data is not possible as, to the best of the authors’ knowledge, no data for the heat release of these cell chemistries were collected at the time of the work.

A common assumption from the literature is that the energy fraction correlates with the mass fraction facilitating the testing procedure [61,81]. Therefore, the energy fractions in Fig. 5 are compared with the mass losses and fractions illustrated in Fig. 7 to prove this assumption.

The results in Fig. 7(b) indicate that the mass fraction of the remaining cell mass is larger than its energy fraction for all investigated battery cells. A better approximation can be achieved by not considering inactive material like the cell can and determining the mass fraction based solely on the mass of the jelly roll in Fig. 7(c). The qualitative results demonstrate that the mass and energy fraction correlate across all investigated cell chemistries. However, in the case of an almost complete jelly roll ejection, as with the NMC 4680 and NFM 18650 cells, larger quantitative errors are to be expected. This reveals that mass loss correlates with the energy released via gas and ejecta. However, mass and energy flow do not necessarily have to be directly proportional, resulting in different mass and energy fractions.

Fig. 5(b and c) also shows that a different burst of the safety vent significantly influences the mass loss and, thus, the energy fraction. This offers margins for the safety design of battery cells, whereby the vent design can control whether the heat should remain in the cell body or be released in the venting channels. Please note that the original function of the safety valve must not be restricted when adapting it. In addition, the same effect can be achieved by changing the cell format, with prismatic NMC-811 cells losing approximately 45.8% [10] and the cylindrical NMC-811 cells from this study losing approximately 75% of their cell mass. As a result, a safety-related cell selection and design in the early phase of battery development can influence where the energy should be released depending on where it can be better compensated in the subsequent safety-related system design to prevent cell-to-cell propagation.

High gas production during thermal runaway can lead to a high convective heat transfer, increased pressure, and a fast accumulation of explosive atmosphere in the battery pack, fostering thermal runaway propagation and making it an essential part of safety assessment [10]. The total gas release is shown for all investigated cells in Fig. 8(a). The normalized gas volume of the NMC-811 cells ranges from 1.71–2.00LAh−1 and is, therefore, in the middle of the range of 1.4– 2.75LAh−1, as known from the literature for NMC cells [61,67,82–85]. The large deviations in the literature can be attributed, on the one hand, to discrepancies in the experimental setup and, on the other hand, to the applied trigger method. Essl et al. [83] investigated the influence of the trigger method on the vent gas emissions of battery cells with NMC-622 cathodes, revealing the highest gas production with overcharging (2.8LAh−1) followed by nail penetration (1.7LAh−1) and the lowest gas production with an overtemperature trigger (1.6LAh−1). The latter corresponds to the trigger method in this study. Therefore, the results represent the lower end of potential gas production, varying strongly with the actual fault case. The LFP cells show a significantly lower gas production of 0.7–1.09LAh−1 compared to the NMC-811 cells, which fits well in the range of 0.57–1.12LAh−1 previously measured in the literature [67,84,85]. The low gas production in LFP cells can be mainly attributed to the gas evolution during the reaction between the electrolyte and anode and the evaporation of the electrolyte [16,67]. Meanwhile, battery cells with NMC cathodes exhibit more reactions with significant gas evolution, such as the release of oxygen during the decomposition of the cathode and its subsequent reaction with the anode and combustible components of the electrolyte [16,24,67,70]. Similar decomposition reactions are suspected for the investigated sodium-ion cell chemistry, whereby the gas production is with 2.08LAh−1, in the range of the NMC-811 cells. The origin of the similar behavior is assumed in the layered metal oxide cathode structure undergoing a phase transition accompanied by an oxygen release and a reaction between cathode and anode at high temperatures, contributing significantly to gas production [77].

Although the LFP cells produce less gas, the safety hazards should not be underestimated, as the gas components are often more explosive, irritating, asphyxiant, and toxic than those of many other lithium-ion and sodium-ion batteries [67,86,87].

Similar to heat release, a cell chemistry independent scaling of the venting gas volume via the gravimetric energy density in Fig. 8(c) is not possible. Since a high gas evolution also tends to transport more material out of the cell, a slightly better estimation can be obtained via the total ejected mass independent of the cell chemistry in Fig. 8(d).

This paper presents a methodology for extensive thermal runaway characterization of lithium-ion and sodium-ion batteries to enable a safety assessment in the early phase of battery development. The investigations focus on the influence of different cell chemistries and energy contents on thermal stability and heat release during thermal runaway using ARC and DATRC measurements. The safety assessment revealed the following results, and can be summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Thermal stability: The thermal stability varies depending on the cell chemistry, whereby the investigated cells can be classified from high to low thermal stability as follows: LFP > NFM > NMC-811. The qualitative reaction dynamics of the two cell chemistries NMC-811 and NFM with layered metal oxide cathodes show a similar behavior. In contrast, the LFP cell chemistries are significantly more stable with their olivine-structured cathodes. Only minor differences could be noticed across the different cell formats of one cell chemistry.

Heat release and fraction: The investigated NMC-811 and NFM cells release more than 87% of the total heat release via gas and ejecta, whereas the same fraction varies significantly between 26 and 88% for the LFP cells. Considering the cell capacity and energy, the normalized heat release allows a classification of the investigated cells from high to low normalized heat release: NMC-811 ≈ NFM > LFP. The total heat release can be scaled to other cell formats via the cell capacity and energy, but the scaling factors depend on the cell chemistry. Scaling independent of the cell chemistry is possible via the total ejected mass under certain conditions.

Mass loss: The mass loss correlates with the energy released via gas and ejecta, but mass and energy flow do not necessarily have to be directly proportional, which can result in differing mass and energy fractions. Furthermore, the mass loss can be influenced by the cell design and format, whereby the energy fraction can also be adjusted to a certain extent.

Venting gas: The venting gas production is comparable for the cell chemistries NMC-811 and NFM with layered metal oxide cathodes and significantly greater than for the investigated LFP cells. However, it should be noted that the safety evaluation is based exclusively on venting gas production. Therefore, the hazards of LFP vent gas should not be underestimated, as according to the literature, the explosiveness or toxicity of the gas is higher compared to the other investigated cell chemistries.

ce:section.ce:para: The results represent a good qualitative comparison of the investigated battery cells and thus provide a valuable basis for cell selection concerning the corresponding application. For further considerations, future research should focus on the statistical analysis of the heat release of different cell chemistries to further substantiate the quantitative results of this study. In addition, a standardized thermal runaway model framework should be developed, which can be used for different cell chemistries and facilitates the transfer of safety properties from the safety assessment. This allows usability beyond the first safety assessment and enables an improvement in the further development steps by accelerating model-based safety-related battery design.
